knowledges.content: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) are autonomous systems powered by large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) designed for automated knowledge discovery and complex problem-solving tasks including coding, web navigation, file processing, and multi-step reasoning.
knowledges.label: dra_definition
knowledges.title: Definition of Deep Research Agents

knowledges.content: The dominant paradigm for improving DRA capabilities focuses on post-training enhancements: supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, and reward modeling, all applied before deployment to update model weights.
knowledges.label: post_training_paradigm
knowledges.title: Dominant post-training paradigm

knowledges.content: The asymmetry of verification (Wei, 2025) states that checking the correctness of a solution is often computationally easier than generating the solution itself. This enables decomposing complex verification problems into simpler, targeted sub-tasks rather than re-solving the full problem.
knowledges.label: asymmetry_of_verification
knowledges.title: Asymmetry of verification principle

knowledges.content: Reflexion-based methods [@Shinn2023] use textual feedback to bootstrap agent responses, but generating high-quality feedback itself requires sophisticated reasoning capability, making naive self-feedback unreliable for DRAs.
knowledges.label: reflexion_background
knowledges.title: Limitation of naive Reflexion-based self-feedback

knowledges.content: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) remain prone to unreliable outputs in long-horizon tasks due to incorrect actions, API failures, hallucinations, and other errors. In tasks spanning dozens of pages and hundreds of actions, online human supervision is infeasible, severely constraining practical deployment.
knowledges.label: dra_unreliability
knowledges.title: DRA reliability problem

knowledges.content: Prior work on inference-time improvement for DRAs has largely emphasized scaling output tokens or selection across parallel rollouts (e.g., parallel sampling for optimal trajectory search, narrative-driven aggregation across iterations [@Zhu2025c]). None of these methods address systematic verification of DRA outputs or structured feedback generation for DRAs.
knowledges.label: test_time_scaling_gap
knowledges.title: Gap in test-time scaling for DRAs

knowledges.content: The generation of structured, high-quality feedback for DRA outputs is a hard task requiring sophisticated reasoning capability. Reflexion-based methods that use raw textual feedback fail to reliably identify subtle reasoning failures, factual errors, or unsupported claims in long-horizon DRA trajectories.
knowledges.label: feedback_generation_is_hard
knowledges.title: Difficulty of structured feedback generation

knowledges.content: Can the asymmetry of verification be exploited to build an automated, decomposition-based verifier that outperforms vanilla LLM-judge and agent-as-judge approaches for DRA outputs?
knowledges.label: rq_verification

knowledges.content: Can iterative verification feedback enable inference-time scaling of DRA performance without requiring additional model training?
knowledges.label: rq_test_time_scaling

knowledges.content: Can fine-tuning on high-quality DRA verification data enable open-source models to develop robust reflection and self-critique capabilities for DRA tasks?
knowledges.label: rq_open_source

knowledges.content: An alternative paradigm to post-training is self-evolving agents: iteratively verifying the policy model's outputs guided by meticulously crafted rubrics derived from a systematic failure taxonomy. This gives rise to inference-time scaling of verification, wherein an agent self-improves by evaluating its generated answers to produce iterative feedback and refinements, without updating model weights.
knowledges.label: alternative_paradigm_claim
knowledges.title: Inference-time scaling of verification paradigm

knowledges.content: This work formalizes the agent reflection pipeline for DRAs and introduces a comprehensive DRA Failure Taxonomy, automatically constructed to categorize agent failures into five major categories and thirteen sub-categories, from which structured rubrics for outcome-based rewards are derived.
knowledges.label: contrib_taxonomy
knowledges.title: Contribution: DRA failure taxonomy

knowledges.content: This work presents DeepVerifier, a rubrics-based outcome reward verifier that leverages the asymmetry of verification and outperforms vanilla agent-as-judge and LLM judge baselines by 12%–48% in meta-evaluation F1 score on DRA verification tasks.
knowledges.label: contrib_deepverifier
knowledges.title: Contribution: DeepVerifier system

knowledges.content: This work demonstrates inference-time scaling of verification that holds for both capable closed-source LLM APIs and supervised fine-tuned open-source models, delivering 8%–11% accuracy gains on challenging subsets of the GAIA benchmark [@Mialon2023] and XBench-DeepSearch [@Chen2025].
knowledges.label: contrib_inference_scaling
knowledges.title: Contribution: inference-time scaling result

knowledges.content: This work releases DeepVerifier-4K, a curated supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset of 4,646 high-quality agent steps focused on DRA verification, emphasizing reflection and self-critique to enable open-source model development.
knowledges.label: contrib_dataset
knowledges.title: Contribution: DeepVerifier-4K dataset

knowledges.content: The WebAggregatorQA dataset [@Wang2025] is used for constructing the DRA failure taxonomy. It exercises core DRA capabilities including multi-step reasoning, multimodal inputs, web browsing, and general tool-use proficiency across 10+ domains. It is selected to avoid data leakage from the evaluation benchmarks (GAIA, BrowseComp, XBench-DeepSearch).
knowledges.label: taxonomy_dataset
knowledges.title: Taxonomy construction dataset: WebAggregatorQA

knowledges.content: Cognitive Kernel-Pro (CK-Pro) [@Fang2025b], a high-performing fully open-source multi-module DRA framework, with Claude-3.7-Sonnet as the backbone model, is used to generate trajectories for taxonomy construction.
knowledges.label: agent_backbone
knowledges.title: Agent and backbone model for trajectory collection

knowledges.content: Two independent research-staff annotators inspect each erroneous trajectory against human reference solution traces from WebAggregatorQA, recording error points (concrete, localized mistakes) with supporting trajectory steps. Sets are reconciled by merging duplicates and retaining distinct items. Average inter-annotator overlap is 63.0%, indicating high agreement.
knowledges.label: annotation_protocol
knowledges.title: Error annotation protocol

knowledges.content: An iterative analysis and labeling process with two annotators with multiple years of AI research experience: initial labels from clustering 50 error points, then iterative refinement comparing/merging labels, removing inadequate categories, and refining definitions.
knowledges.label: taxonomy_construction_method
knowledges.title: Taxonomy construction methodology

knowledges.content: The taxonomy construction corpus comprises 2,997 agent actions across 90 distinct tasks. Trajectory lengths range from 2 to 156 steps (average 33.3 steps). Token counts range from 18.7K to 60.0M tokens (average 8.2M tokens, total 738M tokens). The correct/incorrect ratio is 0.96 (nearly balanced).
knowledges.label: trajectory_corpus
knowledges.title: Trajectory corpus statistics

knowledges.content: DRA trajectories average 8.2M tokens, far exceeding any LLM's context window. This makes direct holistic verification infeasible and necessitates trajectory summarization as a preprocessing step before verification.
knowledges.label: high_token_count
knowledges.title: DRA trajectory token count exceeds LLM context window

knowledges.content: The annotation process yields 555 error points across all incorrect trajectories in the WebAggregatorQA corpus.
knowledges.label: error_points_count
knowledges.title: Total annotated error points

knowledges.content:
The DRA Failure Taxonomy classifies 555 agent failures into five major categories and thirteen sub-categories:

1. **Finding Sources** (most frequent): errors in information acquisition — consulting the wrong evidence, relying on generic searches, secondary-source dependence
2. **Reasoning Failures** (second most common): premature conclusions, misinterpretation, hallucinated or overconfident claims
3. **Problem Understanding and Decomposition**: misunderstanding instructions, goal drift
4. **Action Errors**: UI failures, format mistakes, wrong modality use
5. **Max Step Reached**: cascading early mistakes lead to long, unproductive trajectories

Finding Sources dominates, with the largest flows corresponding to consulting wrong evidence and relying on generic searches — upstream information acquisition is the most frequent failure.
knowledges.label: taxonomy_five_categories

metadata.caption: DRA failure taxonomy categorizing 555 agent failures into five major classes and thirteen subclasses

knowledges.title: DRA Failure Taxonomy: five major categories

knowledges.content: Information acquisition failures (Finding Sources category) dominate DRA failure modes, representing the largest single failure class in the taxonomy. Sub-categories include consulting wrong evidence, relying on generic searches instead of targeted queries, and over-reliance on secondary sources that may be incomplete or inaccurate.
knowledges.label: finding_sources_dominant
knowledges.title: Finding Sources is the dominant failure category

knowledges.content: The DRA Failure Taxonomy directly informs rubric construction for DeepVerifier: each failure category and sub-category corresponds to a dimension in the rubric used to evaluate agent trajectories. The taxonomy provides a principled, data-driven basis for structured verification rather than ad hoc judgment criteria.
knowledges.label: rubrics_from_taxonomy
knowledges.title: Rubrics derived from failure taxonomy

knowledges.content: DeepVerifier adopts a three-stage multi-module architecture: (1) a Decomposition Agent, (2) a Verification Agent, and (3) a Judge Agent. These modules operate sequentially on each DRA trajectory-answer pair.
knowledges.label: three_stage_architecture
knowledges.title: DeepVerifier three-stage architecture

knowledges.content: The CK-Pro agent [@Fang2025b] is used as the Verification Agent in DeepVerifier experiments. CK-Pro is a modular multi-agent framework: a Main Agent orchestrates sub-agents that interact with specific resources (search, screenshot), each generating Python code for actions.
knowledges.label: ck_pro_as_verifier
knowledges.title: CK-Pro as verification agent implementation

knowledges.content: The Judge Agent uses a 4-point scoring scale: 1 = entirely incorrect, 2 = mostly incorrect, 3 = mostly correct, 4 = entirely correct. A score ≤ 2 is treated as incorrect (reject); a score ≥ 3 is treated as correct (accept) in scaling experiments.
knowledges.label: judge_scoring_rubric
knowledges.title: Judge agent 4-point scoring scale

knowledges.content:
The Decomposition Agent operates in three steps:

1. **Trajectory Summarization**: Produces a compact step-indexed synopsis of the trajectory, recording for each step the sources visited and concrete information retrieved (facts, numbers, quotes). Descriptive (not interpretive), enabling downstream checks without reloading the full trace.
2. **Potential Error Identification**: Given the trajectory summary and failure taxonomy in the system prompt, identifies suspicious behaviors mapped to known failure modes as structured behavior⇒potential-error+taxonomy-label pairs with brief justifications.
3. **Follow-Up Question Formulation**: Drafts high-leverage follow-up questions targeting flagged vulnerabilities. Each question is answerable via external evidence and designed to decisively confirm or refute a risky claim. Output is up to 3 source-question pairs.
knowledges.label: decomposition_three_steps
knowledges.title: Decomposition Agent: three-step workflow

knowledges.content: By focusing only on essential, potentially faulty claims, the Decomposition Agent allows the Verification Agent to build on well-grounded conclusions, ignore trivial details, and check only for suspicious or unsupported assertions — avoiding the same reasoning errors as the original DRA agent.
knowledges.label: targeted_subquestions
knowledges.title: Targeted sub-questions avoid re-solving full task

knowledges.content: The Verification Agent sequentially retrieves answers to the follow-up questions generated by the Decomposition Agent. It uses the CK-Pro multi-agent framework, where the Main Agent decomposes each follow-up question into sub-tasks assigned to specialized Sub-Agents (search, screenshot, etc.).
knowledges.label: verification_agent_sequential
knowledges.title: Verification Agent: sequential follow-up answer retrieval

knowledges.content: The Judge Agent evaluates the unverified answer based on: (1) the trajectory summary, (2) the potential error list, (3) the follow-up questions, and (4) their verified answers. It provides a concise explanation followed by a score on the 4-point scale. In feedback mode, the judge also provides actionable retry instructions and may suggest the correct answer if derivable from available information.
knowledges.label: judge_agent_evaluation
knowledges.title: Judge Agent evaluation and feedback generation

knowledges.content:
DeepVerifier (full system) achieves the following meta-evaluation performance on the GAIA-Web verification task (trajectories from CK-Pro with Claude-3.7-Sonnet backbone):

| System | Precision | Recall | Accuracy | F1 |
|--------|-----------|--------|----------|----|
| DeepVerifier (full) | 75.00 | 71.43 | 75.56 | 73.17 |
| − Verification module | 100.00 | 14.29 | 60.00 | 25.00 |
| − Decomposition module | 86.96 | 47.62 | 72.22 | 61.54 |

All values are percentages × 100.
knowledges.label: full_deepverifier_performance
knowledges.title: DeepVerifier ablation: meta-evaluation F1 on GAIA-Web

knowledges.content: Removing the Verification module (no follow-up question answering) yields 100% precision but only 14.29% recall and 60.00% accuracy (F1 = 25.00). The judge catches obvious execution failures but overlooks subtle reasoning or factual errors, accepting many incorrect answers as correct due to inability to detect secondary-source dependence, overconfident claims, or hallucinated facts.
knowledges.label: ablated_no_verification
knowledges.title: Ablation: removal of Verification module

knowledges.content: Removing the Decomposition module (no targeted sub-question generation) yields 86.96% precision but only 47.62% recall and 72.22% accuracy (F1 = 61.54). Without proper decomposition, the verification agent attempts to check every step by re-solving the entire task, making it vulnerable to the same reasoning errors as the original agent.
knowledges.label: ablated_no_decomposition
knowledges.title: Ablation: removal of Decomposition module

knowledges.content: DeepVerifier (full system, F1 = 73.17) achieves a balanced precision-recall tradeoff and outperforms the ablated versions by 12%–48% in meta-evaluation F1 score: +48.17 F1 points over the no-Verification ablation and +11.63 F1 points over the no-Decomposition ablation. DeepVerifier also achieves the highest accuracy (75.56%) among all variants.
knowledges.label: deepverifier_superiority
knowledges.title: DeepVerifier outperforms ablations by 12-48% F1

knowledges.content: Holistic judge without verification (no follow-up question answering): achieves F1 = 25.00 with precision 100.00%, recall 14.29%, accuracy 60.00% on GAIA-Web DRA verification task.
knowledges.label: alt_no_verification_verifier
knowledges.title: Alternative: holistic judge without verification sub-tasks

knowledges.content: Judge with verification but without decomposition: achieves F1 = 61.54 with precision 86.96%, recall 47.62%, accuracy 72.22% on GAIA-Web DRA verification task.
knowledges.label: alt_no_decomp_verifier
knowledges.title: Alternative: verification without decomposition

knowledges.content: DeepVerifier with full decomposition+verification predicts balanced precision-recall (F1 ≈ 73), because decomposition targets only suspicious claims while verification grounds them in evidence.
knowledges.label: pred_deepverifier
knowledges.title: DeepVerifier predicted balanced precision-recall

knowledges.content: Holistic judge without targeted sub-questions predicts high precision but low recall (F1 ≈ 25), because it can only detect obvious errors, missing subtle factual and reasoning failures.
knowledges.label: pred_holistic
knowledges.title: Holistic judge predicted high precision, low recall

knowledges.content:
compare(DeepVerifier with full decomposition+verification predicts balanced precision-recall (F1 ≈ 73), because decomposition targets only suspicious claims while verification grounds them in evidence., Holistic judge without targeted sub-questions predicts high precision but low recall (F1 ≈ 25), because it can only detect obvious errors, missing subtle factual and reasoning failures., DeepVerifier (full system) achieves the following meta-evaluation performance on the GAIA-Web verification task (trajectories from CK-Pro with Claude-3.7-Sonnet backbone):

| System | Precision | Recall | Accuracy | F1 |
|--------|-----------|--------|----------|----|
| DeepVerifier (full) | 75.00 | 71.43 | 75.56 | 73.17 |
| − Verification module | 100.00 | 14.29 | 60.00 | 25.00 |
| − Decomposition module | 86.96 | 47.62 | 72.22 | 61.54 |

All values are percentages × 100.)

Reflective test-time scaling integrates DeepVerifier into the DRA loop: after completing each task, the agent verifies its own outputs using DeepVerifier, collects feedback, and uses it to guide further retries. The feedback loop repeats until the verifier judges the answer correct (score ≥ 3) or a predefined retry limit (10 rounds) is exceeded.
knowledges.label: scaling_protocol
knowledges.title: Reflective test-time scaling protocol

knowledges.content: The corrective feedback prompt instructs the judge to: (1) provide a brief reflection on why the previous answer was wrong, (2) give up to three actionable retry instructions specifying necessary sources and actions, and (3) suggest the correct answer if derivable from available information. Instructions are concise to avoid confusing the agent.
knowledges.label: corrective_feedback_design
knowledges.title: Corrective feedback prompt design

knowledges.content: The GAIA-Web subset of the GAIA benchmark [@Mialon2023] filters for tasks requiring web browsing. The GAIA dataset has three difficulty levels (Level 1 easiest, Level 3 hardest). The GAIA-Full split includes 165 development examples.
knowledges.label: gaia_web_subset
knowledges.title: GAIA benchmark: Web subset and full split

knowledges.content: Main backbone model for scaling experiments: Claude-3.7-Sonnet. Generalization also tested on GPT-4.1 and the fine-tuned DeepVerifier-8B (Qwen3-8B base).
knowledges.label: evaluation_models
knowledges.title: Backbone models evaluated

knowledges.content: On GAIA-Full (n = 165), DeepVerifier with Claude-3.7-Sonnet backbone improves accuracy from 52.22% (0 feedback rounds) to a peak of 60.12% at round 4 (+7.90 points), ending at 58.93% after 10 rounds (+6.71 points final gain). The GAIA-Web subset shows the greatest improvement, rising from 51.11% to a peak of 63.33% at round 4 (+12.22 points), ending at 62.22% (+11.11 points final gain).
knowledges.label: gaia_full_scaling
knowledges.title: GAIA accuracy gains with DeepVerifier scaling (Claude-3.7-Sonnet)

knowledges.content: On GAIA-Full with GPT-4.1 backbone, DeepVerifier improves accuracy from 29.51% (0 rounds) to a peak of 32.53% at rounds 2 and 6 (+3.01 points), ending at 31.92% after 10 rounds (+2.41 points). On GAIA-Web, accuracy rises from 28.89% to a peak of 32.22% (+3.33 points), ending at 31.11% (+2.22 points).
knowledges.label: gaia_gpt41_scaling
knowledges.title: GAIA accuracy gains with DeepVerifier scaling (GPT-4.1)

knowledges.content: On GAIA-Full with DeepVerifier-8B backbone, accuracy improves from 26.73% (0 rounds) to 32.21% after 10 rounds (+5.48 points). On GAIA-Web, accuracy rises from 26.67% to 33.33% (+6.67 points). File/Reasoning/Others subset gains 4.04 points.
knowledges.label: gaia_dv8b_scaling
knowledges.title: GAIA accuracy gains with DeepVerifier-8B scaling

knowledges.content: Performance typically peaks around the fourth feedback round across all models and subsets. This is explained by the interplay of two competing transition rates: the incorrect→correct transition (strong early: 18.99% at round 1, decaying to 0% by round 5) dominates the correct→incorrect regression rate (weaker but persistent: 12.79% at round 1, declining thereafter). Their crossover produces the observed peak around round 4.
knowledges.label: peak_at_round_four
knowledges.title: Scaling peak at round 4 explained by transition rates

knowledges.content:
Transition rates between consecutive feedback rounds on GAIA-Full (Claude-3.7-Sonnet):

| Round | Incorrect→Correct (%) | Correct→Incorrect (%) |
|-------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1     | 18.99               | 12.79               |
| 2     | 9.33                | 4.44                |
| 3     | 6.94                | 4.30                |
| 4     | 8.45                | 1.06                |
| 5     | 0.00                | 3.03                |
| 6     | 1.45                | 0.00                |
| 7–10  | ≤1.45               | ≤1.03               |

The incorrect→correct rate is stronger but decays; the correct→incorrect rate is weaker but persists.
knowledges.label: transition_rates
knowledges.title: Feedback round transition rates

knowledges.content: On XBench-DeepSearch (Chinese multilingual benchmark), DeepVerifier with Claude-3.7-Sonnet backbone improves accuracy from 41.0% (0 rounds) to a peak of 47.0% at round 2 (+6.0 points), ending at 44.0% after 10 rounds (+3.0 points final gain).
knowledges.label: xbench_scaling
knowledges.title: XBench-DeepSearch accuracy with DeepVerifier scaling

knowledges.content: On BrowseComp (extremely hard information retrieval benchmark), DeepVerifier with Claude-3.7-Sonnet backbone improves accuracy from 5.0% (0 rounds) to a peak of 10.0% at rounds 2–3 (+5.0 points), ending at 9.0% after 10 rounds (+4.0 points final gain).
knowledges.label: browsecomp_scaling
knowledges.title: BrowseComp accuracy with DeepVerifier scaling

knowledges.content: The inference-time scaling effect of DeepVerifier generalizes across: (1) model families (Claude-3.7-Sonnet, GPT-4.1, Qwen3-8B/DeepVerifier-8B), (2) dataset types (GAIA-Web for retrieval-heavy tasks; File/Reasoning for non-web tasks; XBench-DeepSearch for multilingual; BrowseComp for extremely hard browsing), confirming that structured rubric-based reflection is a broadly applicable improvement mechanism.
knowledges.label: scaling_generalizes
knowledges.title: Scaling effect generalizes across models and datasets

knowledges.content: Web-based, retrieval-heavy tasks (GAIA-Web subset) benefit most from DeepVerifier's targeted verification and evidence-grounding process: +12.22 points peak gain (Claude-3.7-Sonnet), compared to +2.64 points peak for File/Reasoning/Others subset. This is consistent with Finding Sources being the dominant failure category.
knowledges.label: web_benefits_most
knowledges.title: GAIA-Web benefits most from verification scaling

knowledges.content: Base trajectories for DeepVerifier-4K are collected from 400 DRA runs on WebAggregatorQA [@Wang2025], using CK-Pro with Claude-3.7-Sonnet backbone. Tasks span information aggregation across 10+ domains requiring significant online exploration.
knowledges.label: sft_base_trajectories
knowledges.title: Base trajectory collection for SFT dataset

knowledges.content: Filtering: Only true positive verifications (correctly reject wrong answers) and true negative verifications (correctly accept correct answers) are retained. After balancing these trajectories, verification trajectories are converted to prompt-response pairs.
knowledges.label: sft_filtering_protocol
knowledges.title: SFT data filtering and balancing protocol

knowledges.content: Training configuration for DeepVerifier-8B: Qwen3-8B base model fine-tuned on a mixture of DeepVerifier-4K and the CK-Pro-8B training set from Fang et al. (2025b) [@Fang2025b]. Mixed training preserves foundational capabilities while adding reflection ability.
knowledges.label: sft_training_config
knowledges.title: SFT training configuration for DeepVerifier-8B

knowledges.content: DeepVerifier-4K is constructed from 400 initial DRA verification trajectories (using DeepVerifier with Claude-3.7-Sonnet). After filtering for correctly verified true positives and true negatives and balancing, the dataset contains 4,646 high-quality prompt-response pairs. Examples emphasize reflection and self-critique.
knowledges.label: deepverifier_4k_construction
knowledges.title: DeepVerifier-4K: 4,646 high-quality verification pairs

knowledges.content:
DeepVerifier-8B (Qwen3-8B fine-tuned on DeepVerifier-4K + CK-Pro-8B data) achieves the highest accuracy among open-source models on GAIA-Full after 10 feedback rounds: 32.2% (up from 26.7% at 0 rounds, +5.5 points). This surpasses:
- CK-Pro-8B (CK-Pro training only): gains only 2.6 points with reflection
- Qwen3-8B (no reflection training): shows minimal improvement

Performance by GAIA difficulty level (GAIA-Full, after 10 rounds):

| Level | DeepVerifier-8B | CK-Pro-8B | Qwen3-8B |
|-------|----------------|-----------|----------|
| Level 1 | 40% | 38% | 36% |
| Level 2 | 32% | 29% | 28% |
| Level 3 | 11% | 8% | 8% |
| Average | 34% | 28% | 27% |
knowledges.label: deepverifier_8b_gaia

metadata.caption: Performance comparison after 10 rounds of verification & feedback on GAIA development set

knowledges.title: DeepVerifier-8B vs. other open-source models on GAIA

knowledges.content: Fine-tuning on verification and reflection data (DeepVerifier-4K) is necessary for open-source models to benefit from test-time scaling. DeepVerifier-8B achieves +5.5 points improvement with reflection, while CK-Pro-8B (no reflection data) gains only +2.6 points, and Qwen3-8B (no reflection or CK-Pro training) shows minimal improvement.
knowledges.label: reflection_training_matters
knowledges.title: Reflection training is necessary for open-source scaling benefit

knowledges.content: After fine-tuning on DeepVerifier-4K, open-source models (DeepVerifier-8B) exhibit steady accuracy gains across all 10 feedback rounds on both GAIA subsets: web-based tasks benefit from better fact verification, and file/reasoning tasks reflect improved general reasoning control. This demonstrates that reflection ability generalizes across task types even in 8B-scale models.
knowledges.label: open_source_scaling_law
knowledges.title: DeepVerifier-4K enables open-source scaling across task types

module_titles.motivation: Introduction and Motivation: Inference-Time Scaling of Verification for Deep Research Agents
module_titles.s3_taxonomy: Section 3: DRA Failure Taxonomy — systematic classification of agent failures
module_titles.s4_deepverifier: Section 4: DeepVerifier — three-stage multi-module verification framework
module_titles.s5_scaling: Section 5 & 7.2: Test-Time Scaling with Reflection and Feedback
module_titles.s5_sft: Section 5 & 7.3: DeepVerifier-4K Dataset and Open-Source Model Fine-Tuning

metadata.reason: The combination of @dra_unreliability (practical deployment barrier), @test_time_scaling_gap (existing methods miss DRA verification), and @feedback_generation_is_hard (naive feedback is insufficient) collectively motivates the inference-time verification paradigm (@alternative_paradigm_claim). By exploiting the asymmetry of verification, rubric-guided decomposition addresses the feedback quality problem while enabling test-time improvement [@Wan2026].

@trajectory_corpus establishes that DRA trajectories average 8.2M tokens. @high_token_count states this exceeds any LLM context window, making direct holistic verification infeasible. The infeasibility follows from the quantitative comparison of trajectory token count against practical LLM context window limits (typically 128K–2M tokens).

@error_points_count (555 annotated error points) provides the corpus from which the iterative clustering and labeling procedure (@taxonomy_construction_method) derives the five-category, thirteen-subclass taxonomy (@taxonomy_five_categories). The taxonomy emerges inductively from the empirical error patterns [@Wan2026].

@taxonomy_five_categories explicitly states that Finding Sources has the largest flows and that upstream information acquisition is the most frequent point of collapse. @finding_sources_dominant directly follows from this observation in the taxonomy figure.

The five major categories and thirteen sub-categories of @taxonomy_five_categories are translated into rubric dimensions for @rubrics_from_taxonomy. By exploiting the @asymmetry_of_verification, each rubric dimension targets a specific known vulnerability, making verification tractable by decomposing it into focused sub-checks [@Wan2026].

@high_token_count makes direct holistic verification infeasible, motivating step 1 (trajectory summarization). @taxonomy_five_categories provides the failure mode vocabulary for step 2 (error identification). The asymmetry of verification motivates step 3 (targeted follow-up questions that check only suspicious, specific claims rather than re-solving the full task). Together these constraints determine the three-step design of @decomposition_three_steps [@Wan2026].

@decomposition_three_steps produces yes/no questions of the form 'Does source X state claim Y?' or 'What is the exact figure for Y in report X?' — these are factual lookups, not full re-solves. @targeted_subquestions follows because lookup tasks are substantially easier than the original research task, exploiting @asymmetry_of_verification.

DeepVerifier's F1 (73.17) far exceeds the no-Verification ablation's F1 (25.00), confirming that targeted decomposition-based verification substantially improves recall over holistic judging without sacrificing overall performance. The observed 48% F1 improvement matches the prediction of @pred_deepverifier.

@decomposition_three_steps provides targeted follow-up questions, @targeted_subquestions ensures verification avoids re-solving errors, and @rubrics_from_taxonomy structures the evaluation dimensions. Together these explain the high recall (71.43%) of @full_deepverifier_performance.
@alt_no_verification_verifier (holistic judge) achieves only F1 = 25.00, demonstrating that the full @full_deepverifier_performance cannot be explained by the holistic approach alone.

Both DeepVerifier and the holistic judge attempt to explain the same verification task (GAIA-Web DRA trajectory assessment). @full_deepverifier_performance is the observation that DeepVerifier achieves F1 = 73.17. The hypothesis is that structured decomposition and targeted verification drive this performance; the alternative is that holistic judging alone suffices.

@full_deepverifier_performance provides the numerical basis: DeepVerifier F1 = 73.17, no-Verification F1 = 25.00, no-Decomposition F1 = 61.54. @deepverifier_superiority computes the 12%–48% improvement range from these values.

@transition_rates shows that at round 1, incorrect→correct (18.99%) strongly dominates correct→incorrect (12.79%). By round 4, incorrect→correct (8.45%) still exceeds correct→incorrect (1.06%), but both are declining. Beyond round 4, incorrect→correct reaches 0% while correct→incorrect persists, causing the net improvement to saturate and then slightly reverse — producing the peak at @peak_at_round_four.
@scaling_generalizes asserts broad generalization. @gaia_full_scaling (Claude-3.7-Sonnet, GAIA-Full +7.90 points peak) is the primary evidence confirming the law for the main model.
@scaling_generalizes predicts the improvement holds for GPT-4.1. @gaia_gpt41_scaling confirms +3.01 points on GAIA-Full, providing independent cross-model evidence.
@scaling_generalizes predicts generalization to multilingual datasets. @xbench_scaling confirms +6.0 points on XBench-DeepSearch, providing cross-dataset evidence.
Claude-3.7-Sonnet and GPT-4.1 are independent model families from different organizations
XBench-DeepSearch is a different language/dataset from GAIA, providing independent cross-dataset evidence

@gaia_full_scaling provides the comparative numbers: GAIA-Web peak gain (+12.22) vs File/Reasoning/Others peak gain (+2.64). @web_benefits_most follows because DeepVerifier's follow-up questions primarily target information-retrieval vulnerabilities (Finding Sources category from @rubrics_from_taxonomy), which are more prevalent in web tasks.

@full_deepverifier_performance establishes that DeepVerifier correctly identifies both correct and incorrect DRA answers. The 400 base trajectories from WebAggregatorQA (collected with CK-Pro/Claude-3.7-Sonnet) are filtered by the true-positive/true-negative protocol, yielding @deepverifier_4k_construction with 4,646 pairs. The key insight is that the verifier's correct decisions provide high-quality supervision signal [@Wan2026].

@deepverifier_8b_gaia provides three comparison points: DeepVerifier-8B (+5.5 pts), CK-Pro-8B (+2.6 pts), Qwen3-8B (~0 pts). The monotone relationship between reflection training data (full > CK-Pro-only > none) and scaling benefit supports @reflection_training_matters. The 2x difference (5.5 vs 2.6) between DeepVerifier-8B and CK-Pro-8B isolates the contribution of DeepVerifier-4K specifically.

@deepverifier_8b_gaia shows gains on both GAIA-Web (+6.67 pts) and File/Reasoning/Others (+4.04 pts) subsets, and @reflection_training_matters attributes this to the DeepVerifier-4K training. @open_source_scaling_law follows: the improvement is not task-type specific, indicating that the reflection capability is general [@Wan2026].
